romania.title: Primele explicații ale lui Mihai Jurca după ce a renunțat la televiziune după 21 de ani: „Nu iau niciodată decizii pripite, decizii la nervi, decizii la „cald””

Mihai Jurca a prezentat ultima sa rubrică meteo în televiziune, urmând ca în perioada următoare să se alăture agenției de turism lansate de Răzvan Pascu, în rolul de tour leader. „Am avut mereu …

romania.title: Voluntariat pentru comunitate: UM România dedică a 9-a ediție Impact Day Asociației Șansa Ta
romania.summary: UM România și-a închis din nou birourile pentru a marca cea de-a 9-a ediție a evenimentului global Impact Day, o inițiativă sărbătorită simultan în toate birourile rețelei din întreaga lume. În cadrul inițiativei globale Impact Day, echipa UM s-a alăturat din nou proiectelor comunitare, sprijinind pentru a doua oară Cantina Socială a Asociației Șansa Ta. …

romania.title: După 21 de ani, unul dintre cei mai cunoscuți prezentatori meteo renunță la televiziune și anunță o nouă destinație. „Drumul spre televiziune va fi înlocuit de drumul spre aeroport”
romania.summary: Mihai Jurca se desparte de Antena 1 și intră într-o nouă industrie. După mai bine de 20 de ani în care a prezentat știri și rubrica meteo, Mihai Jurca renunță la televiziune. Fiul uneia dintre legendele televiziunii din România, Romica Jurca, și ea prezentatoare și realizatoare de emisiuni meteo, Mihai Jurca intră oficial în industria …

Potrivit judecătorilor, constructorul lăcaşului de cult ilegal are la dispoziţie 60 de zile să îl mute ilegal în altă rezervaţie naturală, preferabil în alt judeţ, ca să fie problema ălora […] The post Justiţia a decis: biserica ridicată ilegal la Techirghiol trebuie mutată ilegal în altă zonă naturală protejată appeared first on Times New Roman .

Împreună cu alţi colegi, Ciolacu şi-a amintit cu nostalgie de primii paşi pe culoarele şcolii, când era doar un ţânc de 32 de ani şi nu-şi […] The post Ciolacu, emoţionat la întâlnirea de 20 de ani: Nu-mi vine să cred că în 20 de ani termin liceul! appeared first on Times New Roman .

Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: Don't blame the block: Study finds nerve blocks alone don't increase pain sensitivity in healthy volunteers
medical.summary: Nerve blocks—local anesthetic injections that block pain signals—are a valuable opioid-sparing technique for controlling pain after surgery. However, some patients experience temporary sharp increases in pain as the nerve block wears off. This phenomenon, often termed "rebound pain," does not result from nerve hypersensitivity to painful testing (e.g., heat, pressure, punctate pain), reports a healthy volunteer study in the August issue of Anesthesiology.

medical.title: Muscles matter for diabetes risk, new study finds
medical.summary: A major new international study led by Curtin University has found diabetes risk is about more than body weight or obesity, revealing that muscle health also likely plays a major role in whether people develop the condition.

medical.title: Mediterranean diet compounds may aid nerve cells as brains age, review finds
medical.summary: Natural plant compounds known as polyphenols, found in berries, tea, cocoa, coffee and extra-virgin olive oil, may positively influence biological processes linked to Alzheimer's disease and other disorders characterized by the gradual loss of nerve cells, according to a review by researchers at Semmelweis University. The analysis, published in Nutrients, suggests that these bioactive compounds may contribute to healthy brain aging.

medical.title: KRAS-targeting drugs show promise for rare appendix cancer
medical.summary: Targeting mutant KRAS may offer a promising new treatment option for appendix cancer, according to research from The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center published in the Journal of Hematology & Oncology.

medical.title: Chronic consumption of xanthan gum may cause inflammation in the colon
medical.summary: Xanthan gum is found almost everywhere. This food additive has thickening, stabilizing and gelling properties and is one of the food industry's most widely used additives for adjusting the consistency of ice cream, yogurt, cakes, sauces and gluten-free pasta. It is also used to prepare beverages for people with dysphagia, or difficulty swallowing.

medical.title: Rapid magnetic brain stimulation eases depression within days, but benefits fade within weeks
medical.summary: Despite decades of advances in mental health care, depression remains one of the world's most disabling conditions. Many people with major depressive disorder (MDD) fail to find lasting relief from antidepressants or psychological therapies. Studies indicate that the global burden is expected to rise, with more than 466 million people projected to be living with depressive disorders by 2040.

medical.title: Student-led study aims to improve diagnosis of early-onset dementia, a frequently misdiagnosed condition
medical.summary: Research led by a University of Sydney medical student could enable earlier and more accurate diagnosis of behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia—one of the most commonly misdiagnosed forms of early-onset dementia, which can begin as early as a person's late 40s and is often mistaken for a psychiatric condition.

medical.title: Unpaid caregivers shoulder more dementia costs than health systems in high-income Europe
medical.summary: Researchers found that 58% of total dementia-related costs are linked to unpaid care provided by family members, friends and other informal caregivers, highlighting the substantial burden of dementia beyond health care systems alone. Notably, unpaid informal care (€128 billion annually) exceeded direct health care costs (€93 billion), underscoring the often-hidden contribution made by families and caregivers.

medical.title: What positive change have you seen unfold? We want to know
medical.summary: Share the stories of change you’ve witnessed that took patience, persistence and time The post What positive change have you seen unfold? We want to know appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Revealed: the world’s most liveable cities in 2026
medical.summary: Asian cities have shot up the latest Global Liveability Index, which has just been released. Where does your city rank? The post Revealed: the world’s most liveable cities in 2026 appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: A vitamin A discovery is changing what scientists know about vision
medical.summary: A surprising discovery is reshaping scientists' understanding of how humans develop sharp central vision before birth. Instead of blue cone cells migrating away from the retina's center, the study found they transform into red and green cones under the influence of vitamin A-related signals and thyroid hormones. The findings could improve lab-grown retinal tissue and lay the groundwork for future cell therapies to restore vision lost to age-related eye diseases.

medical.title: Brain activity under anesthesia challenges what we know about consciousness
medical.summary: The unconscious brain appears to be far more capable than scientists once believed. Researchers found that patients under general anesthesia could still process language at a sophisticated level, distinguishing nouns, verbs, and adjectives while listening to stories. Even more remarkably, neural activity showed signs of predicting upcoming words before they were heard. The results challenge traditional ideas about consciousness and hint at new possibilities for brain-computer interfaces.

science.title: Researchers define new frontier in quantum materials
science.summary: Researchers at City College of New York physicist Vinod M. Menon's Laboratory for Nano and Micro Photonics (LaNMP) have outlined an emerging frontier in quantum materials: atomically thin systems in which light, magnetism and electric charge are strongly intertwined. This rapidly evolving field could enable next-generation optoelectronic and quantum technologies leveraging the coupled dynamics of light, charge and spin.

science.title: Researcher creates seawater isotope database to improve climate data reconstructions and projections
science.summary: A Florida State University paleoclimatologist led the creation of a global database hosting thousands of seawater isotope measurements collected over almost 50 years that will aid scientists in generating more accurate climate reconstructions and predictions.

science.title: Highly filled liquid epoxy for smaller, more reliable chip packaging
science.summary: As computer chips become more powerful and compact, the materials that protect them must perform better than ever. In advanced chip packaging, liquid epoxy is widely used because it can flow into tiny spaces before curing into a solid protective layer. To be effective, the material must be easy to process in its liquid state while becoming strong, stable and reliable after curing.

science.title: Well-designed urban street plantings provide relief from summer heat
science.summary: New research shows that improvements in tree, shrub and grass plantings along city streets in Queensland can substantially improve pedestrians' thermal comfort.

science.title: Disturbance has a greater effect on giant kelp productivity than resource availability
science.summary: Marine scientists at UC Santa Barbara have found that disturbances to giant kelp forests have a major influence on their net primary productivity (NPP)—an indicator of an ecosystem's health and its ability to support its community—even more so than the availability of the resources needed for growth. Using data from decades of monthly measurements in the kelp forests at the campus's National Science Foundation-supported Santa Barbara Coastal Long Term Ecological Research (SBC LTER) site, the researchers uncovered the direct, indirect and fascinating effects of disturbance on one of the world's most productive ecosystems.

science.title: Study reveals Hawaiian hotspot is getting hotter
science.summary: Contrary to conventional geological thinking, the Hawaiian mantle plume has gotten hotter by about 250°C (480°F) over the past 47 million years. This discovery, led by Earth scientists at the University of Hawai'i at Mānoa, reverses the long-held idea that hotspots start out very hot and progressively cool over time. The study, published recently in Earth and Planetary Science Letters, also found that heat surges produced the two largest volcanoes along the Northwestern and Main Hawaiian Island chain.

science.title: Underwater microphones detect dolphins in Sea of Japan bays about once every ten days
science.summary: When residents of two coastal communities along the Sea of Japan reported observing dolphins in nearby waters, it caught the attention of some scientists. The marine environment has been changing rapidly in the Sea of Japan, which is partially enclosed by the Japanese archipelago and the Eurasian continent and connected to the ocean through shallow straits. Though dolphin sightings and bycatch records have been reported along the coast, basic ecological information on small odontocetes, such as dolphins and porpoises, has remained limited.

science.title: Shear loading reveals sixfold damage growth around stiff particles in aluminum alloy
science.summary: The resistance of materials to mechanical loads is a decisive factor in component safety, such as in aircraft. Working as part of an international team, researchers from the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT) have found a previously unknown damage mechanism in metals: Contamination in the form of stiff particles can cause the volume of voids to increase up to sixfold when exposed to deformation by shear loading.

science.title: Space cargo costs could fall more than 90% by 2040, study suggests
science.summary: The expense of launching cargo into space will plummet over the next few years, with the cost of reaching orbit forecast to more than halve between now and the end of the decade, and fall by around 93% by 2040, according to new Cambridge-led research.

science.title: Frame-dragging observations validate Einstein yet again
science.summary: More than a century after Albert Einstein first transformed our understanding of gravity, his general theory of relativity continues to withstand ever more demanding experimental tests. Now, an international team led by Ignazio Ciufolini at the Chinese Academy of Sciences has carried out the most precise measurement yet of one of the theory's most subtle predictions: the dragging of spacetime caused by Earth's rotation.

science.title: Weather it together: Farmers speaking up about mental health
science.summary: Rain or shine, Aussie farmers and farming communities have always looked out for one another. But when it comes to their own mental health, asking for help hasn't always come easily.

science.title: Scientists finally solved a 150-year-old gallium mystery
science.summary: Scientists have rewritten the story of gallium after discovering that its unusual atomic bonds re-form at high temperatures, contradicting decades of accepted theory. The finding changes how researchers explain why the metal melts so easily and behaves unlike almost any other metal. Beyond solving a long-standing scientific mystery, the work could lead to advances in semiconductors, nanotechnology, and liquid metal engineering.

science.title: These ancient quasars shouldn't exist so soon after the Big Bang
science.summary: Astronomers have uncovered 31 of the oldest known quasars, including the two earliest ever detected, shining from a time when the universe was only about 670 million years old. Powered by supermassive black holes billions of times the Sun’s mass, these incredibly bright objects challenge scientists’ understanding of how such enormous black holes formed so quickly after the Big Bang.

science.title: These tiny soil microbes could rescue crops from salty farmland
science.summary: Researchers have discovered that beneficial soil bacteria give plants an unexpected survival advantage in salty soils. Instead of helping plants keep salt out, the microbes stimulate the production of lignin, a natural compound that strengthens roots and makes plants more resilient. Greenhouse and field tests showed healthier plants and higher yields in salty conditions. The findings could lead to bio-based treatments that help farmers grow crops on land once considered too salty for agriculture.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.summary: Cunoscutul prezentator al rubricii meteo de la Antena 1 a explicat pentru PaginadeMedia ce se află în spatele schimbării sale de carieră. Mihai Jurca a prezentat ultima sa rubrică meteo în televiziune, urmând ca în perioada următoare să se alăture agenției de turism lansate de Răzvan Pascu, în rolul de tour leader. „Am avut mereu …

mix_items.title: După 21 de ani, unul dintre cei mai cunoscuți prezentatori meteo renunță la televiziune și anunță o nouă destinație. „Drumul spre televiziune va fi înlocuit de drumul spre aeroport”
mix_items.summary: Mihai Jurca se desparte de Antena 1 și intră într-o nouă industrie. După mai bine de 20 de ani în care a prezentat știri și rubrica meteo, Mihai Jurca renunță la televiziune. Fiul uneia dintre legendele televiziunii din România, Romica Jurca, și ea prezentatoare și realizatoare de emisiuni meteo, Mihai Jurca intră oficial în industria …

mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Don't blame the block: Study finds nerve blocks alone don't increase pain sensitivity in healthy volunteers
mix_items.summary: Nerve blocks—local anesthetic injections that block pain signals—are a valuable opioid-sparing technique for controlling pain after surgery. However, some patients experience temporary sharp increases in pain as the nerve block wears off. This phenomenon, often termed "rebound pain," does not result from nerve hypersensitivity to painful testing (e.g., heat, pressure, punctate pain), reports a healthy volunteer study in the August issue of Anesthesiology.

mix_items.title: Researchers define new frontier in quantum materials
mix_items.summary: Researchers at City College of New York physicist Vinod M. Menon's Laboratory for Nano and Micro Photonics (LaNMP) have outlined an emerging frontier in quantum materials: atomically thin systems in which light, magnetism and electric charge are strongly intertwined. This rapidly evolving field could enable next-generation optoelectronic and quantum technologies leveraging the coupled dynamics of light, charge and spin.

mix_items.title: Researcher creates seawater isotope database to improve climate data reconstructions and projections
mix_items.summary: A Florida State University paleoclimatologist led the creation of a global database hosting thousands of seawater isotope measurements collected over almost 50 years that will aid scientists in generating more accurate climate reconstructions and predictions.

mix_items.title: Highly filled liquid epoxy for smaller, more reliable chip packaging
mix_items.summary: As computer chips become more powerful and compact, the materials that protect them must perform better than ever. In advanced chip packaging, liquid epoxy is widely used because it can flow into tiny spaces before curing into a solid protective layer. To be effective, the material must be easy to process in its liquid state while becoming strong, stable and reliable after curing.

mix_items.title: Muscles matter for diabetes risk, new study finds
mix_items.summary: A major new international study led by Curtin University has found diabetes risk is about more than body weight or obesity, revealing that muscle health also likely plays a major role in whether people develop the condition.

mix_items.title: Well-designed urban street plantings provide relief from summer heat
mix_items.summary: New research shows that improvements in tree, shrub and grass plantings along city streets in Queensland can substantially improve pedestrians' thermal comfort.

mix_items.title: Mediterranean diet compounds may aid nerve cells as brains age, review finds
mix_items.summary: Natural plant compounds known as polyphenols, found in berries, tea, cocoa, coffee and extra-virgin olive oil, may positively influence biological processes linked to Alzheimer's disease and other disorders characterized by the gradual loss of nerve cells, according to a review by researchers at Semmelweis University. The analysis, published in Nutrients, suggests that these bioactive compounds may contribute to healthy brain aging.

mix_items.title: Disturbance has a greater effect on giant kelp productivity than resource availability
mix_items.summary: Marine scientists at UC Santa Barbara have found that disturbances to giant kelp forests have a major influence on their net primary productivity (NPP)—an indicator of an ecosystem's health and its ability to support its community—even more so than the availability of the resources needed for growth. Using data from decades of monthly measurements in the kelp forests at the campus's National Science Foundation-supported Santa Barbara Coastal Long Term Ecological Research (SBC LTER) site, the researchers uncovered the direct, indirect and fascinating effects of disturbance on one of the world's most productive ecosystems.

mix_items.title: Study reveals Hawaiian hotspot is getting hotter
mix_items.summary: Contrary to conventional geological thinking, the Hawaiian mantle plume has gotten hotter by about 250°C (480°F) over the past 47 million years. This discovery, led by Earth scientists at the University of Hawai'i at Mānoa, reverses the long-held idea that hotspots start out very hot and progressively cool over time. The study, published recently in Earth and Planetary Science Letters, also found that heat surges produced the two largest volcanoes along the Northwestern and Main Hawaiian Island chain.

mix_items.title: KRAS-targeting drugs show promise for rare appendix cancer
mix_items.summary: Targeting mutant KRAS may offer a promising new treatment option for appendix cancer, according to research from The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center published in the Journal of Hematology & Oncology.

mix_items.title: Chronic consumption of xanthan gum may cause inflammation in the colon
mix_items.summary: Xanthan gum is found almost everywhere. This food additive has thickening, stabilizing and gelling properties and is one of the food industry's most widely used additives for adjusting the consistency of ice cream, yogurt, cakes, sauces and gluten-free pasta. It is also used to prepare beverages for people with dysphagia, or difficulty swallowing.

mix_items.title: Underwater microphones detect dolphins in Sea of Japan bays about once every ten days
mix_items.summary: When residents of two coastal communities along the Sea of Japan reported observing dolphins in nearby waters, it caught the attention of some scientists. The marine environment has been changing rapidly in the Sea of Japan, which is partially enclosed by the Japanese archipelago and the Eurasian continent and connected to the ocean through shallow straits. Though dolphin sightings and bycatch records have been reported along the coast, basic ecological information on small odontocetes, such as dolphins and porpoises, has remained limited.

mix_items.title: Rapid magnetic brain stimulation eases depression within days, but benefits fade within weeks
mix_items.summary: Despite decades of advances in mental health care, depression remains one of the world's most disabling conditions. Many people with major depressive disorder (MDD) fail to find lasting relief from antidepressants or psychological therapies. Studies indicate that the global burden is expected to rise, with more than 466 million people projected to be living with depressive disorders by 2040.

mix_items.title: Shear loading reveals sixfold damage growth around stiff particles in aluminum alloy
mix_items.summary: The resistance of materials to mechanical loads is a decisive factor in component safety, such as in aircraft. Working as part of an international team, researchers from the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT) have found a previously unknown damage mechanism in metals: Contamination in the form of stiff particles can cause the volume of voids to increase up to sixfold when exposed to deformation by shear loading.

mix_items.title: Student-led study aims to improve diagnosis of early-onset dementia, a frequently misdiagnosed condition
mix_items.summary: Research led by a University of Sydney medical student could enable earlier and more accurate diagnosis of behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia—one of the most commonly misdiagnosed forms of early-onset dementia, which can begin as early as a person's late 40s and is often mistaken for a psychiatric condition.

mix_items.title: Space cargo costs could fall more than 90% by 2040, study suggests
mix_items.summary: The expense of launching cargo into space will plummet over the next few years, with the cost of reaching orbit forecast to more than halve between now and the end of the decade, and fall by around 93% by 2040, according to new Cambridge-led research.

mix_items.title: Frame-dragging observations validate Einstein yet again
mix_items.summary: More than a century after Albert Einstein first transformed our understanding of gravity, his general theory of relativity continues to withstand ever more demanding experimental tests. Now, an international team led by Ignazio Ciufolini at the Chinese Academy of Sciences has carried out the most precise measurement yet of one of the theory's most subtle predictions: the dragging of spacetime caused by Earth's rotation.

mix_items.title: Unpaid caregivers shoulder more dementia costs than health systems in high-income Europe
mix_items.summary: Researchers found that 58% of total dementia-related costs are linked to unpaid care provided by family members, friends and other informal caregivers, highlighting the substantial burden of dementia beyond health care systems alone. Notably, unpaid informal care (€128 billion annually) exceeded direct health care costs (€93 billion), underscoring the often-hidden contribution made by families and caregivers.

mix_items.title: Weather it together: Farmers speaking up about mental health
mix_items.summary: Rain or shine, Aussie farmers and farming communities have always looked out for one another. But when it comes to their own mental health, asking for help hasn't always come easily.

mix_items.title: What positive change have you seen unfold? We want to know
mix_items.summary: Share the stories of change you’ve witnessed that took patience, persistence and time The post What positive change have you seen unfold? We want to know appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Revealed: the world’s most liveable cities in 2026
mix_items.summary: Asian cities have shot up the latest Global Liveability Index, which has just been released. Where does your city rank? The post Revealed: the world’s most liveable cities in 2026 appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solved a 150-year-old gallium mystery
mix_items.summary: Scientists have rewritten the story of gallium after discovering that its unusual atomic bonds re-form at high temperatures, contradicting decades of accepted theory. The finding changes how researchers explain why the metal melts so easily and behaves unlike almost any other metal. Beyond solving a long-standing scientific mystery, the work could lead to advances in semiconductors, nanotechnology, and liquid metal engineering.

mix_items.title: A vitamin A discovery is changing what scientists know about vision
mix_items.summary: A surprising discovery is reshaping scientists' understanding of how humans develop sharp central vision before birth. Instead of blue cone cells migrating away from the retina's center, the study found they transform into red and green cones under the influence of vitamin A-related signals and thyroid hormones. The findings could improve lab-grown retinal tissue and lay the groundwork for future cell therapies to restore vision lost to age-related eye diseases.

mix_items.title: These ancient quasars shouldn't exist so soon after the Big Bang
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have uncovered 31 of the oldest known quasars, including the two earliest ever detected, shining from a time when the universe was only about 670 million years old. Powered by supermassive black holes billions of times the Sun’s mass, these incredibly bright objects challenge scientists’ understanding of how such enormous black holes formed so quickly after the Big Bang.

mix_items.title: Brain activity under anesthesia challenges what we know about consciousness
mix_items.summary: The unconscious brain appears to be far more capable than scientists once believed. Researchers found that patients under general anesthesia could still process language at a sophisticated level, distinguishing nouns, verbs, and adjectives while listening to stories. Even more remarkably, neural activity showed signs of predicting upcoming words before they were heard. The results challenge traditional ideas about consciousness and hint at new possibilities for brain-computer interfaces.

mix_items.title: These tiny soil microbes could rescue crops from salty farmland
mix_items.summary: Researchers have discovered that beneficial soil bacteria give plants an unexpected survival advantage in salty soils. Instead of helping plants keep salt out, the microbes stimulate the production of lignin, a natural compound that strengthens roots and makes plants more resilient. Greenhouse and field tests showed healthier plants and higher yields in salty conditions. The findings could lead to bio-based treatments that help farmers grow crops on land once considered too salty for agriculture.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am setat alarma la 6:00. Ea a venit, eu am dat delegare.

satire_ro.title: Justiţia a decis: biserica ridicată ilegal la Techirghiol trebuie mutată ilegal în altă zonă naturală protejată
